ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hot pot food product class (FOODON_00002473); no Sichuan mala-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Sichuan mala hotpot-specific code. A0CKP (hot pot dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for Sichuan mala hotpot as a food product.

main_ingredients: various dipping ingredients (meat, tofu, vegetables, noodles)

serving.description: 1 serving of hotpot broth with mixed dipping ingredients
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sichuan mala hotpot is approximately 500 g, comprising roughly 200 ml of mala soup base broth consumed plus an assortment of dipped and cooked ingredients such as thinly sliced beef, tofu, leafy vegetables, and noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Spicy Soup (Mala Tang) / Sichuan Hotpot — composite nutrient estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef tallow, chilli-based broth, and mixed hotpot ingredients; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Asian soup dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Sichuan mala hotpot as a complete dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for beef tallow-based broth, dried chilli, Sichuan peppercorn, and representative mixed dipping ingredients (beef, tofu, leafy vegetables, noodles). Values represent the edible portion of a typical mixed serving normalised to 100 g. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Asian spicy soup dishes used for cross-validation. Confidence is medium due to high variability in restaurant preparation and ingredient ratios.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Asian soup and hotpot dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for Asian noodle soup dishes and mixed meat-vegetable preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sichuan mala hotpot in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous entries for mixed Asian soup dishes with noodles, meat, and vegetables (GI range 35–55 in the Sydney database). The low GI reflects the mixed macronutrient composition, high protein and fat content from beef and tallow, and moderate carbohydrate from noodles and vegetables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 26.0) / 100 = 11.7, using 500 g serving with 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~2600 mg per 500 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Sichuan mala hotpot due to its very high sodium content from the mala broth, fermented bean paste, and dipping sauces, which can substantially exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~11.7 per serving) with variable carbohydrate from noodles and starchy ingredients
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Sichuan mala hotpot in moderation, choosing lean protein and non-starchy vegetable dipping ingredients while limiting noodles and starchy items to manage postprandial blood glucose; the low GI of approximately 45 is favourable, but portion size and carbohydrate selection require careful management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from beef tallow broth and elevated sodium; potential trans fat from tallow
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with Sichuan mala hotpot due to its high saturated fat content from beef tallow-based broth (~3.1 g per 100 g), elevated sodium (~520 mg per 100 g), and trace trans fat, all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; opting for a lighter broth base and lean protein dipping ingredients is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sichuan mala hotpot (四川麻辣火锅, Sìchuān Málà Huǒguō) is a communal Chinese cooking dish originating from Sichuan province, characterised by its intensely spicy, numbing, and aromatic broth made from beef tallow, dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, doubanjiang (fermented broad bean chilli paste), garlic, ginger, and a blend of spices including star anise, cinnamon, and bay leaf. The defining sensory experience is the 'mala' (麻辣) combination: 'má' (numbing) from Sichuan peppercorns and 'là' (spicy) from dried chillies. Diners cook a wide variety of raw ingredients — thinly sliced beef, lamb, pork, seafood, tofu, leafy vegetables, mushrooms, and noodles — directly in the simmering broth at the table. The dish is highly variable in nutritional composition depending on the ingredients selected. Per 100 g of a typical mixed serving, it provides approximately 112 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 6.8 g fat (of which 3.1 g is saturated, primarily from beef tallow), and 5.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notably high at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to the broth and fermented condiments. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), reflecting the mixed macronutrient composition. Clinically, the dish poses a high sodium burden relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, and moderate glycemic load relevant to Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, Sichuan mala hotpot is widely available at Chinese restaurants and hotpot chains, and has become a popular dining format. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets in its traditional form due to pork-derived ingredients and beef tallow.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sichuan hotpot?
Is mala hotpot high in sodium?
What is the GI of hotpot?
What is in mala soup base?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sichuan mala hotpot as a complete dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients and cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Asian spicy soup dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to high variability in restaurant preparation, ingredient ratios, and the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component source data for this dish type and is not a characteristic nutrient of hotpot broth or typical dipping ingredients. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. GI is an analogous estimate at 45 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed Asian soup and noodle dishes; no direct GI measurement for Sichuan mala hotpot exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 5.2 × 500/100) / 100 = (45 × 26.0) / 100 = 11.7. Sodium content is highly variable depending on broth concentration, dipping sauce use, and ingredient selection; the value of 520 mg per 100 g represents a moderate-to-high estimate for a typical restaurant preparation. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite hotpot dish. The cuisine has been corrected from 'Global' to 'Chinese' reflecting the dish's Sichuan origin. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this dish is served in Singapore context. The input restaurant tag 'Flavours by Saute - Funan' is noted but not used as a nutritional source per source policy.

Corrected cuisine from 'Global' to 'Chinese' reflecting Sichuan origin.
